name: Charlotte, North Carolina
tagline: North Carolina's largest city (~900K) and the Southeast's banking hub: Charlotte runs a $3.6B FY2026 budget, and its central Innovation & Technology (I&T) department drives an AI roadmap, smart-city work, and the city-run cooperative purchasing alliance.
overview: The City of Charlotte is North Carolina's largest municipality, with roughly 900,000 residents and a metro economy anchored in banking and finance. The city operates under a council-manager form of government: an elected Mayor and City Council set policy, and a professional City Manager (Marcus D. Jones) runs day-to-day operations across departments. Central technology sits with the Innovation & Technology (I&T) department, led by CIO and Director Markell Storay, which owns enterprise infrastructure, applications, data and analytics, cybersecurity, and the city's smart-city and innovation programs. Charlotte is a mature, ambitious technology buyer for a municipality — it has published a Technology Strategic Plan and a 2025–2028 AI Strategic Roadmap, runs the long-standing Smart Charlotte initiative, and operates its own cooperative purchasing program (the Charlotte Cooperative Purchasing Alliance) that other public entities can buy through. For a Dell SLG seller, Charlotte is best understood as a large, self-directed local buyer with its own procurement vehicles, layered on top of North Carolina's statewide and cooperative contracts — the city's own I&T leadership, not a state agency, owns most technology decisions.

budget.intro: Charlotte adopted a $3.6B balanced FY2026 budget (effective July 1, 2025) with no property-tax increase, alongside a FY2026–2030 Capital Investment Plan. The city does not publish a single headline IT figure the way the topline budget reads, so technology spend is best tracked through the I&T department budget and capital/technology line items rather than one number — confirm current figures in the adopted budget before forecasting.

stats.label: FY2026 adopted budget
stats.note: Balanced; no property-tax increase; effective July 1, 2025.

stats.label: Residents
stats.note: Largest city in North Carolina; drives the scale of enterprise IT and services.

stats.label: Capital Investment Plan
stats.note: Multi-year CIP adopted with the FY2026 budget; technology and facilities capital sit here.

notes: FY2027 update: on June 8, 2026, Council adopted a ~$4.5B FY2027 budget (9-2) with the city's first property-tax increase in years — 1.89 cents (to 29.3 cents per $100) dedicated to public safety, funding 10% raises for police and firefighters — plus a FY2027–2031 Capital Investment Plan. Refresh the topline and CIP references against the FY2027 adopted budget.
Charlotte funds operations across a general fund plus enterprise funds (water, aviation/airport, etc.); some technology spend rides those enterprise budgets, not just the general fund.
Treat any specific I&T or technology line figure as provisional until confirmed against the adopted budget document.

initiatives.name: AI Strategic Roadmap (2025–2028)
initiatives.summary: I&T has published a multi-year AI Strategic Roadmap covering governance, use cases, and responsible adoption across city services. This is the live window for AI-infrastructure, data-platform, and governance positioning — map Dell's AI compute and partner stack to the roadmap's priorities.

initiatives.name: Data & analytics modernization
initiatives.summary: The city has invested in data and analytics leadership (including a Chief Data/Analytics role) and lists Data & Analytics among its technology strategic priorities. A route for data-platform, storage, and analytics infrastructure tied to citywide decision-making.

initiatives.name: Smart Charlotte / smart-city program
initiatives.summary: Smart Charlotte is the city's long-running smart-city initiative focused on connectivity, digital inclusion, and using operational data to improve services. Edge, networking, and IoT-adjacent infrastructure plays attach here — confirm current active projects before pitching.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity & technology resiliency
initiatives.summary: I&T lists technology resiliency among its strategic priorities, covering security posture and continuity for a large municipal enterprise. Security, backup/recovery, and resilient-infrastructure messaging fit here — confirm the current security leadership and standards before engaging.

procurement.intro: Selling to Charlotte runs on the city's own procurement plus the state and cooperative vehicles layered around it. Confirm first how Dell is reached for a given buy: directly via City of Charlotte procurement, through the city-run Charlotte Cooperative Purchasing Alliance, or via NC statewide/cooperative contracts.

vehicles.name: City of Charlotte Procurement Services
vehicles.detail: The city's central contracting and procurement function runs solicitations (RFPs/IFBs) and manages city contracts — the direct path for Charlotte-specific technology buys.

vehicles.name: Charlotte Cooperative Purchasing Alliance (CCPA)
vehicles.detail: A cooperative purchasing program established and administered by the City of Charlotte; contracts are competitively solicited and awarded by the city and open to other public entities. A potential lead-state-style co-op route — confirm which categories apply.

vehicles.name: NC statewide & IT term contracts
vehicles.detail: As an NC public entity, Charlotte can leverage North Carolina statewide term contracts (DOA/P&C for non-IT, NCDIT for IT). Confirm Dell coverage and whether a state vehicle is the cleaner path for a given offering.

vehicles.name: National cooperatives (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint, NCPA/Sourcewell-type)
vehicles.detail: Large municipalities often buy off national cooperative contracts to avoid one-off RFPs. Verify which national co-ops Charlotte uses and where Dell is positioned before recommending a path.

powerStructure.name: Marcus D. Jones
powerStructure.title: City Manager, City of Charlotte
powerStructure.note: Chief executive of city operations under the council-manager form; owns department direction including I&T. Presents the annual budget to Council (proposed the ~$4.5B FY2027 budget in May 2026). Senior relationship for the enterprise motion. Confirmed in role as of June 2026.

powerStructure.name: Rebecca Hefner
powerStructure.title: Deputy City Manager, City of Charlotte
powerStructure.note: Named Deputy City Manager in May 2026 (13-year city veteran); succeeds retiring Deputy City Manager Liz Babson (retirement effective Sept 2026). A senior operations relationship layered under the City Manager — confirm her portfolio/departments before engaging.

powerStructure.name: Markell Storay
powerStructure.title: CIO & Director, Innovation & Technology (I&T)
powerStructure.note: Leads central IT — infrastructure, applications, data, security, and innovation. Longtime city technologist (former CTO) who succeeded Reenie Askew. The top technology relationship for Charlotte.

powerStructure.name: I&T — Data & Analytics leadership
powerStructure.title: Chief Data / Analytics officer (city)
powerStructure.note: Charlotte stood up dedicated data/analytics leadership under I&T; owns the data-platform and analytics agenda tied to the AI roadmap. Confirm the current officeholder before engaging.

powerStructure.name: I&T — Cybersecurity / security leadership
powerStructure.title: City security lead (CISO-equivalent)
powerStructure.note: Owns municipal security posture and standards within I&T — the gate for security on cloud and infrastructure deals. Public naming is limited; confirm the current security leader before engaging.

powerStructure.name: City of Charlotte — Procurement Services
powerStructure.title: Central procurement / contracting
powerStructure.note: Owns city solicitations, contract vehicles, and the Charlotte Cooperative Purchasing Alliance — the access path onto Charlotte's own contracts.

news.title: Council adopts ~$4.5B FY2027 budget (9-2) with first property-tax increase in years
news.impact: Fiscal posture shifts: a 1.89-cent tax increase (to 29.3 cents) plus 10% public-safety raises signal spending priorities tilt toward public safety — technology competes harder for dollars, so lead with efficiency, consolidation, and measurable value. Refresh proposals against the FY2027–2031 CIP.

news.title: City Manager Jones proposes $4.5B FY2027 budget; names new Deputy City Manager Rebecca Hefner
news.impact: Budget cycle anchored by the City Manager (spring proposal, June adoption) — engage ahead of the spring window. Hefner (13-year city veteran) succeeds retiring Deputy City Manager Liz Babson (Sept 2026); a new senior operations relationship to map.

news.title: CIO Storay requires all I&T staff to earn AI and cloud certifications amid on-prem-to-cloud shift
news.impact: Strong buying signal: the city is actively migrating from on-premises infrastructure to cloud and upskilling staff in AI, Python, cybersecurity, and analytics. Position hybrid-cloud, AI-infrastructure, and migration/advisory plays tied to the I&T modernization and AI roadmap.

news.title: Charlotte City Council unanimously adopts $3.6B FY2026 budget with no property-tax increase
news.impact: Prior-year baseline: a balanced, no-tax-increase FY2026 envelope — useful contrast to the FY2027 tax increase when framing fiscal trajectory.

news.title: City Manager Marcus D. Jones presents proposed FY2026 budget to Mayor and Council
news.impact: Annual budget cycle anchored by the City Manager; engage ahead of the spring proposal window to influence priorities.

news.title: I&T publishes AI Strategic Roadmap (2025–2028)
news.impact: Defines the city's multi-year AI direction and governance — early window to position AI-infrastructure and responsible-AI offerings.

dellActions: Confirm how Dell reaches Charlotte for each buy — direct via City Procurement Services, through the Charlotte Cooperative Purchasing Alliance, or via NC statewide/national cooperative contracts — and validate Dell's coverage on each.
Lead AI-infrastructure and data-platform positioning tied to the I&T AI Strategic Roadmap (2025–2028) and the city's Data & Analytics priorities.
Build the executive map: City Manager Marcus D. Jones for enterprise direction and CIO Markell Storay (I&T) as the primary technology relationship.
Attach networking, edge, and resilient-infrastructure plays to Smart Charlotte and the I&T technology-resiliency priority — confirm current active projects first.
Time proposals to Charlotte's budget cycle (City Manager's spring proposal, June adoption) and align to the FY26–30 Capital Investment Plan rather than mid-year asks.
